reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 11 )
reports.phase: Plugging — USDW protection plug set across freshwater zone
reports.summary: Another full day of regulator-witnessed sealing work, with CalGEM inspectors on site throughout. The crew ran tubing back in and confirmed the top of the previous cement plug at 2,602 ft, then filled the section from 2,602 ft up to 1,640 ft with heavy abandonment mud — a dense fluid that holds pressure in the space between cement plugs. With CalGEM approval, they perforated (punched holes in) the casing right at the underground source of drinking water (USDW, about 1,635 ft) so cement could be forced behind the pipe to protect that freshwater zone. They then set the next cement plug from 1,640 ft up to 1,089 ft and squeezed additional cement behind the casing. The well was left shut in under 500 psi overnight, with CalGEM signing off on each step.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 10 )
reports.phase: Plugging — second cement plug set
reports.summary: A big day of sealing work, all witnessed by state regulators (CalGEM). The crew first pressure-tested the well casing to 510 psi for 30 minutes — it held, confirming the pipe is sound. With CalGEM approval, they punched small holes (perforations) in the casing at about 2,950 ft so cement could reach the space behind the pipe, then confirmed cement from the previous plug at 3,091 ft. They pumped the second major cement plug, filling the well from 3,091 ft up to about 2,471 ft, and squeezed additional cement behind the casing. The well was left shut in under pressure overnight with CalGEM sign-off on each step.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 9 )
reports.phase: Plugging — first cement plug set
reports.summary: The crew finished cleaning sand and debris out of the bottom of the well, reaching its full depth of 3,624 ft. CalGEM inspectors then came out and approved both the safety equipment (blowout preventer) and the cleaned-out depth. With an inspector watching, the crew pumped the first cement plug, sealing the well from the bottom (3,624 ft) up to about 3,000 ft. This is the first of several plugs that will permanently seal the well from bottom to top.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 8 )
reports.phase: Obstruction cleared — junk recovered
reports.summary: Breakthrough day. The blockage at 3,338 ft that stalled the job for several days turned out to be a piece of old equipment (a pipe collar with a plug) left in the well years ago. When the crew pulled their grinding tool (taper mill) out of the hole, the junk came out with it. With the obstruction gone, they were able to run back down past the old blockage point, reaching 3,552 ft, and began cleaning out the remaining sand toward the bottom of the well at 3,624 ft.
reports.full_text: Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Run in hole with 10 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 3338', Discuss with E&B rep, Rig up power swivel & established circulation ( Fluid level @ 1970' ) Mill from 3338' to 3340' & unable to make more hole ( Note: While Milling lost 280 bbls of fluid, Make a 45 viscosity pill & spot pill downhole ) Discuss with E&B rep, Rig out power swivel, Pull out hole with 105 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing, 7-1/2'' string mill, 2-7/8'' x 2-3/8'' crossover & 3-1/4'' taper mill ( Note: Taper mill came out with a fish " 3-1/2'' collar with bull plug " ) Discuss with E&B rep, Decision was made to run in hole with 1-5/8'' tubing, Pick up 11 jts of 1-5/8'' coarse 6rd tubing, 1-5/8'' coarse x 2-3/8'' eue cross over, 2-3/8'' eue x 2-7/8'' eue cross over, Run in hole with 102 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well at 3552', TD @ 3624', 72' of fill to clean out, Make up king swivel & established circulation ( Fluid level @ 49' ) Clean out fill from 3552' to 3568' ( Note: Fine sand on returns ) Circulated hole clean 1 hole volume ( 200 bbls ) Pull out hole with 20 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & set tubing tail @ 2937', Close in & secure well overnight, End of shift.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 7 )
reports.phase: Troubleshooting obstruction — milling
reports.summary: The crew kept working on the blockage at 3,338 ft. They first ran a magnet down the well to fish out any loose metal — it came back empty. They then switched to a tapered grinding tool (taper mill) and managed to grind through about 2 ft of the obstruction before stopping for the day, losing some working fluid into the formation in the process. Progress was slow but the team was closing in on the cause.
reports.full_text: Travel to Huntington Beach Lease, Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Discuss with E&B rep, Make up 3-1/4'' magnet, Run in hole with 11 jts of 2-3/8'' eue 8rd tubing, 2-3/8'' x 2-7/8'' Crossover, 95 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 3338', Pull out hole with 95 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing, 2-7/8'' x 2-3/8'' crossover, 11 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & 3-1/4'' magnet ( Note: No shard was present in the magnet ) Discuss with E&B rep, Decision was made to run in hole with taper mill, Make up 3-1/4'' taper mill, Run hole with 1 jt of 2-3/8'' eue 8rd tubing, 2-3/8'' x 2-7/8'' crossover, 7-1/2'' string mill, 105 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 3338', Rig up power swivel & established circulation, Mill from 3338' to 3340' ( Note: While Milling lost 80 bbls of fluid ) Discuss with E&B rep, Rig out power swivel, Pull out hole with 10 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & set tubing tail @ 3045', Close in & secure well overnight, End of shift.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 6 )
reports.phase: Troubleshooting obstruction
reports.summary: Still working the blockage at 3,338 ft. The crew swapped to a different bottom-end tool (mule shoe) and tried for two hours to drill or wash through the obstruction with no success. The report notes possible damage in the well at that depth. The team conferred with the E&B representative and secured the well for the night to plan the next approach.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 5 )
reports.phase: Troubleshooting obstruction — drilling attempt
reports.summary: The crew brought in a small drill bit (3-inch tricone) to try to drill through the hard blockage at 3,338 ft. After two hours of drilling with no progress, they stopped — whatever is at that depth would not drill out, suggesting possible damage or debris in the well rather than ordinary sand fill. The well was secured overnight while the team planned a different approach.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 4 )
reports.phase: Casing inspection (CBL) + obstruction found
reports.summary: The crew ran a scraper tool to clean the inside of the casing, then brought in a wireline company to run a Cement Bond Log — an acoustic survey that checks how well the existing cement is bonded to the casing from 3,320 ft to surface. This log is required by CalGEM to plan the plugging. Afterward, the crew hit something solid at 3,338 ft that would not wash or rotate through, with clean returns indicating it was not sand — the first sign of the obstruction that took several more days to resolve.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i ( Day 3 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout
reports.summary: First full work day back after the holiday weekend. The crew ran pipe into the well and found sand fill starting at 3,260 ft. They circulated fluid to wash out about 76 ft of sand, locating the top of the well's inner liner pipe at 3,336 ft. The hole was flushed clean with roughly 400 barrels of fluid and the well was secured for the night. This cleanout is standard preparation before the well can be inspected and plugged.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-6 ( Day 5 ) Job Complete
reports.phase: Final surface plug — abandonment complete
reports.summary: Final day on A-6. With a CalGEM inspector witnessing, the crew placed the last cement plug from 120 ft to the surface, permanently sealing the top of the well. They then removed the safety equipment, rigged down, and cleaned up the location. The well is now fully plugged and abandoned with no accidents reported on the job.

Job Complete   " No Accidents "

reports.subject: Angus # A-6 ( Day 4 ) — EWS Well Crew Report
reports.phase: Plugging — upper plugs set and mud placed to near surface
reports.summary: The crew tagged cement at 2,366 ft and CalGEM inspector Brandon Martin approved abandonment mud placed from there up to 1,610 ft. CalGEM inspector Jennifer Michel then witnessed a 93-cubic-foot cement plug pumped from 1,610 ft up to about 1,339 ft. After waiting three hours for the cement to harden, the crew confirmed the plug top right at 1,339 ft, and inspector Martin approved a second column of abandonment mud from 1,339 ft up to 120 ft — leaving only the surface plug for the next day. The well was secured overnight.

Under a CalGEM-approved proposal, wireline perforated the casing from 2,650 to 2,670 ft across the top of the oil zone. After tagging cement at 2,793 ft, CalGEM inspector Amanda Seckington witnessed a 166-cubic-foot plug pumped from 2,793 ft up to about 2,307 ft, followed by a 19-cubic-foot squeeze into the annulus behind the casing. The well was shut in at 500 psi with her approval.

reports.subject: Angus # A-6 ( Day 2 ) — EWS Well Crew Report
reports.phase: Plugging — BOP retest passed and first plug set
reports.summary: The crew tagged the well bottom at 3,258 ft and circulated the hole clean. The morning blowout-preventer inspection failed due to a leaking valve on the accumulator unit, so a replacement was delivered and CalGEM inspector Daniel Dennis passed the retest that afternoon — safety equipment must be fully functional before plugging can begin. He then witnessed the first cement plug placed in two stages, 86 cubic feet filling the well from 3,258 ft up to about 2,700 ft. The well was secured overnight.

reports.subject: Angus # A-6 ( Day 1 ) — EWS Well Crew Report
reports.phase: Rig-up — production equipment removed and cement bond log run
reports.summary: The rig moved from A-5 to A-6. The crew opened the well, confirmed no hazardous gases, removed the production tree, and installed and function-tested the blowout preventer. All the work tubing was pressure-tested to 5,000 psi as it was run back in the hole, and every joint passed. The crew tagged the bottom of the well at 3,820 ft and secured it overnight.

reports.subject: Angus # A-17 ( Day 1 ) — EWS Well Crew Report
reports.phase: Rig-up — well opened and production equipment removed
reports.summary: The crew moved onto the A-17 location, set up the workover rig, and lined the site with protective plastic. They opened the well and checked the air for hazardous gases (none present), then removed the surface pumping equipment and pulled 109 sucker rods and the downhole pump out of the well. A blowout preventer (BOP) — the safety valve stack that seals the well during work — was installed and function-tested. They then pulled all 105 joints of production tubing and ran back in with a casing scraper to clean the pipe walls, tagging the top of the liner at 3,330 ft before securing the well for the night.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-8i  ( Day 12 )  Job Complete
reports.phase: Surface plug set — well plugged & abandoned (Job Complete)
reports.summary: This was the final day of work on A-8i, and the well is now permanently sealed. The crew ran tubing back in and confirmed the top of the last cement plug at 1,337 ft, which CalGEM inspector Art Willoughby witnessed and approved at 8:18 AM. They then filled the wellbore from 1,337 ft up to 120 ft with heavy abandonment mud (a dense fluid that holds back any remaining pressure between plugs), also witnessed and approved by CalGEM. Finally, Excalibur's cementers placed the surface cement plug from 120 ft all the way to ground level — the last barrier required to fully abandon the well — with CalGEM witnessing the placement at 12:50 PM. The crew then removed the blowout preventer, took down the rig, and cleaned up the location. The job is complete with no accidents, meaning A-8i is now a fully plugged and abandoned well.

Job Complete   " No Accidents "

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 1 )
reports.phase: Rig-up — wellhead removed, tubing hanger freed
reports.summary: This was the first day of plug-and-abandonment work on A-7i. After the crew's safety meeting, the workover rig and equipment were moved onto the location, the surface flow lines were locked out, protective plastic was laid down, and the rig was rigged up and guyed in place; the well was opened and the air checked for H2S and other hazardous gases, with none present. The crew removed the wellhead's injection tree and flow lines, but found the 'donut locking rams' (the wellhead components that lock the tubing in place) seized from corrosion and had to replace five damaged ones. They then worked on freeing the tubing hanger (the fitting that suspends the well's internal pipe): an initial pull of 60,000-80,000 lbs would not release it, so after consulting the E&B representative they gradually increased the pull in 10,000-lb steps until it came free at 110,000 lbs. The well was closed in and secured for the night.
reports.full_text: Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Move rig & equipment to Angus # A-7i, L.O.T.O flow lines, Install plastic on location, M.I.R.U & guy out rig, Off load tools & equipment, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Remove grating from cellar, Remove injection tree & flow lines, Nipple down hub clamps, Unscrew donut locking rams ( Note: Donut locking rams were seized up due corrosion / rust, Change out 5 donut locking rams due damage ) Single out rig, Attempt to un-flanged tubing hanger 60K-80K with no success, Discuss with E&B rep, Decision was made to continue working tubing hanger, Worked tubing hanger free @110K ( Note: Started working tubing hanger @ 80K & increase 10K increments until tubing hanger came free @ 110K ) Close in & secure well overnight, End of shift.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 2 )
reports.phase: Stuck tubing — freepoint run and tubing cut
reports.summary: The crew's central problem today was that the well's internal production tubing was stuck and would not pull free. After confirming no hazardous gases were present, they repeatedly tried to work the pipe loose by pulling on it with 50,000 to 90,000 lbs of force, attempted to circulate fluid and pressure up the well, and installed and function-tested the blowout preventer (the well-control safety stack) — but the tubing still would not move. They then called in Raptor Wireline to run a 'free-point' survey, a downhole tool that pinpoints exactly where the pipe is stuck: it showed the tubing completely free down to about 2,510 ft but fully stuck at 2,711 ft. Based on that reading, the crew ran a jet cutter and severed the tubing at 2,540 ft, then pulled 76 joints (roughly 2,500 ft) of the freed pipe out of the hole. The well was closed in and secured overnight; this remains early rig-up and troubleshooting work, not yet the cement-plugging phase.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 3 )
reports.phase: Fishing — overshot run, failed to latch fish; possible casing damage
reports.summary: The crew continued fishing operations to recover the section of production tubing that was cut and left stuck in the well the previous day. They ran a fishing tool (an "overshot," designed to slide over and grip the top of the stuck pipe) into the hole, first cleaning out 148 ft of fill and debris that had settled on top of the fish and circulating the well clean with about 309 barrels of fluid. They then made several attempts to latch onto the stuck pipe but could not get a solid grip — the tool would catch only lightly (a "friction bite"), hold about 20,000 lbs of pull, then slip free. After the repeated failed attempts, the crew and the E&B representative decided to pull the tool string back out of the hole and inspect it; the guide shoe came up showing external wear that may indicate damage to the well's casing. The well was closed in and secured overnight — no cement plugs were set and the job is not complete.

reports.source: Daily Rig Report email from Jorge Macias (JMacias@ewscorp.net), msg 19eb51aea061767e

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i   ( Day 4 )
reports.phase: Fishing — fish recovered, wellbore cleanout begun
reports.summary: Today the crew finally recovered the stuck pipe (the "fish") that had stalled the job for two days. They ran a fishing tool called an overshot — a device that slides over the top of the stuck pipe and grips it — down to 2,539 ft, latched onto the fish, and then "jarred" on it for two hours, repeatedly delivering 110,000-lb upward impacts with a downhole jarring tool until the pipe broke free. As they pulled the recovered tubing out, they found 22 joints packed solid with fine sand; they cleaned out 21 of them, laid aside one that was plugged solid, and recovered about 21 cubic feet of sand. With the obstruction gone, the crew ran back in with a clean-out assembly, tagged sand fill at 2,578 ft and located the top of the well's inner liner at 3,317 ft — leaving 739 ft of fill still to remove — then washed out the first stretch from 2,578 to 2,666 ft before securing the well overnight. This is still cleanout and troubleshooting work; no cement plugs have been set and the job is not complete.
reports.full_text: Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Make up 5-3/4'' overshot with 6-1/8'' guide shoe & loaded with 2.875'' grapple, 4-3/4'' Bumper Sub, 4-3/4'' jars, ( 4 ) 4-3/4'' drill collars, 4-3/4'' accelerator & 3-1/2'' IF x 2-7/8'' eue cross over, Run in hole with 67 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 2539', Latch on to fish, Jar on fish for 2-hrs @ 110K & successfully freeing " fish ", Discuss with E&B rep, Pull out hole with 67 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & Lay down BHA, Pull out hole with 22 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd open ended tubing ( Note: 22 jts of 2-7/8'' tubing was full of fine sand,  Was able to clean out 21 jts of 2-7/8'' tubing, Lay down 1 jts of 2-7/8'' tubing plugged up, Recover 21 cu.ft of fine sand )  Discuss with E&B rep, Make up 2-7/8'' mule shoe, Run in hole with 78 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 2578', T.O.L @ 3317', 739' of fill to clean out, Make up king swivel & established circulation, Circulated fill out from 2578' to 2666', Circulated hole clean 1 hole volumes ( 162 bbls ) Rig out king swivel, Pull out hole with 14 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & set tubing tail @ 2203', Close in & secure well overnight, End of shift.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 5 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout — washing out sand fill; lost circulation, recovered fine sand
reports.summary: The crew kept cleaning sand and debris out of the well after the previous day's fishing job. They ran clean-out tubing back in and tapped the top of the fill at 2,666 ft, then pumped fluid down the pipe to wash the fill upward and out of the hole. As they worked from 2,666 to 2,732 ft the well "lost circulation" — about 100 barrels of fluid leaked off into the formation instead of returning to surface — so they mixed a thicker "viscosity pill" (a high-viscosity slug of mud) to plug the loss zone and got circulation going again, with the fluid standing 985 ft down the hole. They cleaned out fill down to 2,996 ft but the well plugged off again; after conferring with the E&B company representative they pulled the clean-out string out of the hole, recovering about 6 cubic feet of fine sand on the bottom-hole tool. The well was closed in and secured for the night and the crew returned to the Bakersfield yard. This is continued cleanout work — no cement plugs have been set and the well is not yet plugged and abandoned.
reports.full_text: Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Run in hole with 67 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 2666', Make up king swivel & established circulation, Circulated fill out from 2666' to 2732' & lost circulation ( 100 bbls ) Make a 45 viscosity pill, Established circulation ( Fluid Level @ 985' ) Clean out fill from 2732' to 2996' & got plugged up, Discuss with E&B rep, Rig out king swivel, Pull out hole with 90 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & 2-7/8'' mule shoe ( Note: Recover 6 cu.ft of fine sand ) Close in & secure well overnight, Travel to Bakersfield yard, End of shift.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 6 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout — sand fill returned over weekend; recovered fine sand via vacuum truck
reports.summary: After the weekend break, the crew returned to find that sand had kept sloughing back into the well: they ran 86 joints of tubing back in and tapped the top of the fill at 2,844 ft, meaning roughly 152 ft of new sand had settled in over the two days off. After conferring with the E&B company representative, they pulled the string partway out and repositioned the open end of the tubing (the "tubing tail") at 2,203 ft, then closed in and secured the well. They brought in a TMG vacuum truck to clean out the mud pump and surface equipment, recovering 66 barrels (about 370 cubic feet) of the fine sand that has been the recurring obstacle on this well. The job is still in the wellbore-cleanout phase, with the well refilling with fine sand faster than it can be permanently cleared.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 7 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout — washing sand fill; severe lost circulation (320 bbls), pulled out of hole
reports.summary: The crew continued trying to clean sand fill out of the well. They ran tubing back in and tapped the top of the fill at 2,844 ft, then pumped fluid down to wash the fill upward and out of the hole. As they cleaned out from 2,844 to 2,930 ft and again from 2,930 to 3,028 ft, the well repeatedly "lost circulation" — the pumped fluid leaked off into the formation instead of returning to surface, about 120 barrels each time and 320 barrels lost for the day. The crew mixed thicker "viscosity pills" (high-viscosity mud slugs) to try to seal off the loss zone, but could not re-establish circulation. After conferring with the E&B company representative, they pulled the entire tubing string — 91 joints plus the mule-shoe bottom tool — back out of the hole and secured the well overnight. This is still wellbore-cleanout work; no cement plugs have been set and the well is not yet plugged and abandoned.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 8 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout — tubing-bailer runs recovered 32 cu.ft fine sand; casing began flowing a small stream
reports.summary: The crew switched tactics to attack the recurring sand problem, bringing in a "tubing bailer" — a hollow tool run on the end of the tubing that scoops up loose sand from the bottom of the hole and is then pulled to surface and emptied. They tapped the top of the sand fill at 3,005 ft and located the top of the casing liner at 3,317 ft, leaving 312 ft of sand to clean out. Over four bailing runs they slowly worked the fill down — 3,005 to 3,025 ft, 3,025 to 3,035 ft, 3,035 to 3,044 ft, and 3,040 to 3,042 ft — recovering fine sand each trip (8, 14, and 10 cubic feet on the first three runs, 32 cu.ft total for the day), but each run stalled because they could not make further hole. On the final run they noticed the casing began flowing a small stream of fluid at surface. After conferring with the E&B company representative, the crew closed in and secured the well overnight. This remains wellbore-cleanout work ahead of plugging; no cement plugs have been set and the well is not yet plugged and abandoned.

reports.subject: Daily Rig Report Angus # A-7i ( Day 9 )
reports.phase: Wellbore cleanout — 97 ft of sand sloughed back overnight; tubing stuck and worked free
reports.summary: The sand problem persisted overnight. When the crew ran tubing back in, they found that 97 ft of sand had sloughed back into the hole, so they tagged fill at 2,947 ft — higher than where they left off. They mixed a thick fluid (a "viscosity pill") to help carry sand to surface, established circulation, and washed the fill down from 2,947 ft to 3,094 ft, but the tubing became stuck in the hole. The crew worked it free, losing 180 barrels of fluid into the formation while fine sand returned to surface, and noted the tubing dragged ("pulling tight") between 3,094 ft and 2,830 ft — a sign the hole remains unstable. They pulled back, set the tubing tail at 2,036 ft, and secured the well overnight. This is still wellbore-cleanout work ahead of plugging; no cement plugs have been set and the well is not yet plugged and abandoned.
reports.full_text: Held safety meeting with crew & filled out JSA, Service rig & equipment, Open well & monitor atmosphere for H2S & other hazardous gases none present, Make up 2-3/8'' saw tooth collar & pick up 1 jt of 2-3/8'' eue 8rd tubing, Run in hole with 88 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & tag well @ 2947', 97' of fill came in overnight, Discuss with E&B rep, Make a 45 viscosity pill, Make up king swivel & established circulation, Circulate fill out from 2947' to 3094' & got stuck, Worked tubing free ( Note: Lost 180 bbls, Returns are fine sand ) Discuss with E&B rep, Pull out hole with 36 jts of 2-7/8'' eue 8rd tubing & set tubing tail @ 2036' ( Note: Pulling tight 3094' to 2830' ) Discuss with E&B rep, Close in & secure well overnight, End of shift.

A-7I.story_so_far: A-7i has been on the rig since June 8 (Day 9 as of June 18). The first four days went to rigging up, then freeing and cutting stuck tubing and fishing the cut tubing back out of the hole — with possible casing damage noted on Day 3. Since Day 5 the crew has fought the same problem for five straight days: fine sand keeps sloughing back into the wellbore faster than it can be cleaned out, repeatedly costing circulation (over 300 bbl lost on Day 7 alone) and sticking the tubing. No cement has been set yet — the well is still being cleaned out in preparation for plugging.
A-7I.plan_context: Against the approved rev3.0 program, the crew is still on Step 3 of 23 — clean out and reach TD at 3,793 ft before any cement goes in. The deepest they have reached is about 3,094 ft: roughly 80 ft below the top of the Lower Bolsa (3,017 ft), but still about 100 ft above the Ashton (3,197 ft) and ~700 ft short of TD. The recurring lost circulation lines up with the Bolsa hydrocarbon zones and the top perforation (3,443 ft MD / 3,007 ft TVD) taking fluid. Nothing can be plugged until they tag TD and run the CBL (3,200–1,300 ft); the first cement plug then sets from 3,793 ft up to 3,097 ft — 100 ft above the Ashton.
